asteroid.name: 99942 Apophis
asteroid.named_after: Apophis (Apep), the ancient Egyptian serpent deity of chaos

discovery.location: Kitt Peak National Observatory, Arizona, USA
discovery.telescope: 2.3-meter Bok telescope with Steward Observatory 90prime camera

physical.composition: Stony — silicate minerals (olivine, pyroxene), metallic iron; similar to LL chondrite meteorites

close_approach_2029.comparison: Closer than geostationary satellites, ~10x closer than the Moon

history.note: 2029 impact ruled out; brief concern for 2036 keyhole
All scenarios eliminated through 2116 via radar

missions.name: OSIRIS-APEX
objectives: Map shape and surface changes from tidal forces
Perform gas thruster surface disturbance experiment

missions.name: RAMSES
missions.full_name: Rapid Apophis Mission for Space Safety
objectives: Monitor real-time tidal deformation during flyby
Compare before/after surface and shape changes

fun_facts: Apophis will be visible to the naked eye during closest approach — a rare event for an asteroid
The asteroid will move across the sky fast enough to track with the unaided eye
Earth's gravity will change Apophis's orbital class from Aten to Apollo
The close approach will shorten Apophis's orbital period from 323 to 307 days
At closest approach, Apophis will be moving at about 7.4 km/s relative to Earth
The 2029 flyby will be visible from about 2 billion people across Europe, Africa, and Asia
Apophis will briefly become brighter than the stars in the Little Dipper
The asteroid's tumbling rotation is expected to change significantly due to Earth's tidal torques
